summary:
In normal and inflamed conditions, mast cells persistently reside in dental pulp where they play roles in immune response mechanisms irrespective of specific pathological statuses. Their continuous presence underscores an integral function beyond just reactive phases associated strictly with inflammation onset or progression.
Mast cells remain constant inhabitants of dental pulp under various physiological circumstances including health and disease due to their involvement in general immunological functions rather than exclusively localized reactions tied directly to acute inflammations. To summarize:

- Mast cells exist stably throughout different states of oral tissue integrity from healthy to diseased.
- They participate broadly in maintaining local defense systems against potential threats like infections despite varying degrees of surrounding cellular activity indicative of either homeostasis or active pathology such as inflammation. This summary captures the essence while adhering closely to your instructions for brevity focusing only on critical aspects pertinent to understanding why mast cells' role is significant independent of immediate signs of inflammation. Agent2 concludes by emphasizing two main points about mast cells’ persistence and broad participation in defending tooth structures whether normally intact or when facing challenges represented through chronic versus transient forms of irritation/infection leading up towards more severe pathologies if left unchecked over time impacting overall periodontology stability negatively otherwise maintained via natural protective measures facilitated largely thanks to resident populations comprising diverse leukocyte subsets notably inclusive amongst others those specialized granule-containing elements known collectively referred-to here simply as 'mastcells'.
